coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and chromatographic isolation of blue, green, and orange emissive carbon dots with high quantum yields from Taraxacum
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Liao, Shihua
Wang, Jiayang
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials with superior optical properties and biocompatibility compared to traditional quantum dots. This study demonstrates a facile approach for synthesizing high quantum yield tricolor CDs from the natural precursor dandelion via a one-step solvothermal treatment. Chromatographic separation successfully yielded blue, green, and orange emissive CDs with emission peaks at 481 nm, 530 nm, and 587 nm respectively. Structural and optical characterization revealed the CDs possess excellent photostability, salt resistance, and thermal stability, alongside high quantum yields, measured at 43.8 %, 32.9 %, and 48.1 % for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, respectively. Taking advantage of the unique properties of the tricolor CDs, their application in analytical sensing was explored using metal ion detection. The CDs displayed selective quenching responses towards Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+ respectively, with strong anti-interference capabilities. This work provides a sustainable and efficient strategy for producing high-performance multicolor CDs, holding significant implications for reducing the synthesis cost of carbon dots and expanding their application spectrum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Taraxacum
Chromatographic separation
Multicolor carbon dots
Ion detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis of tricolor CDs.
Impact of Raw Materials. Comparisons of (a) fluorescence spectra, (b) fluorescence intensity and (c) maximum emission wavelength, respectively.

Structural Characterization. TEM images of the (a) B-CDs, (b) G-CDs and (c) O-CDs respectively. The insets in each TEM image are the corresponding HR-TEM and histogram of the particle size distribution; FT-IR spectra of the (d) B-CDs, (e) G-CDs and (f) O-CDs respectively.
Compositional Characterization. (a–c) XPS survey spectra; high-resolution XPS of (d–f) C1s spectra, (g–i) O1s spectra, and (j–l) N1s spectra for B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs, respectively.

Optical characteristics of the multicolor carbon dots. (a-c) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra, (d-f) three-dimensional contour spectra, (g-i) UV–visible absorption spectra (purple), excitation spectra (blue), emission spectra, (j-l) time-resolved fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs, respectively.

Excitation dependent Emissions of tricolor CDs. (a-c) Fluorescence spectra and (d-f) their correscponding normalized fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs at different excitation wavelengths, respectively.
Impact of Solvents on the Luminescence Properties of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs. (a–c) Fluorescence spectra, (d–f) fluorescence intensity, (g–i) maximum emission wavelength.
Calculation of Quantum Yields for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs. (a–c) Plots of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance in different solvents. (d–f) Quantum yields in different solvents.
Salt Resistance and photostability. (a) The NaCl concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity. (b) The time-dependent fluorescence intensity.
Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs at different temperatures (a–c), and their fluorescence intensity variation curves with Temperature (d).
Impact of time on the luminescence properties of tricolor CDs. (a–c) Comparison of fluorescence spectra of CDs after one week of storage for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, respectively.
Metal ion detection. Fluorescence spectra of tricolor CDs in reaction with different ions (a–c), variations in relative fluorescence intensity I/I 0 (d–f), and anti-interference capabilities (g–i).
Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and O-CDs at different concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+, respectively (a–c) and their corresponding Stern-Volmer equation fitting lines, respectively(d-f).

UV absorption spectra (a) and infrared spectra (d) of B-CDs, Fe3+ and Fe3+@B-CDs, UV absorption spectra (b) and infrared spectra (e) of G-CDs, Pb2+ and Pb2+@G-CDs, UV absorption spectra (c) and infrared spectra (f) of O-CDs, Sn4+ and Sn4+@O-CDs.
Comparison of quantum yields of carbon dots derived from natural products.
Comparison for the proposed method for the detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+ with previous study.
Ion detection in actual water samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and chromatographic isolation of blue, green, and orange emissive carbon dots with high quantum yields from Taraxacum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu, 610225, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu, 610225, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials with superior optical properties and biocompatibility compared to traditional quantum dots. This study demonstrates a facile approach for synthesizing high quantum yield tricolor CDs from the natural precursor dandelion via a one-step solvothermal treatment. Chromatographic separation successfully yielded blue, green, and orange emissive CDs with emission peaks at 481 nm, 530 nm, and 587 nm respectively. Structural and optical characterization revealed the CDs possess excellent photostability, salt resistance, and thermal stability, alongside high quantum yields, measured at 43.8 %, 32.9 %, and 48.1 % for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, respectively. Taking advantage of the unique properties of the tricolor CDs, their application in analytical sensing was explored using metal ion detection. The CDs displayed selective quenching responses towards Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+ respectively, with strong anti-interference capabilities. This work provides a sustainable and efficient strategy for producing high-performance multicolor CDs, holding significant implications for reducing the synthesis cost of carbon dots and expanding their application spectrum.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial that has attracted considerable research attention, owing to the continuous development of new materials [1,2]. CDs are a new class of fluorescent nanomaterials composed of ultra-small carbon nanoparticles typically less than 10 nm in size, which possess excellent optical properties [3,4]. Luminescent nanomaterials, including semiconductor quantum dots, polymer dots, nanoclusters, and CDs, have been extensively explored by scientists for important applications in optoelectronics, biomedicine, and other areas, due to their favorable properties. In particular, CDs have garnered substantial interest due to their pronounced advantages in terms of environmental friendliness and stability. In the past few years, significant progress has been made in understanding the synthesis, characteristics, and applications of CDs through in-depth studies. Currently, CDs play a vital role in diverse areas including materials fabrication, biomedical imaging, chemical sensing, photocatalytic reactions, and numerous other fields [5–8].

The synthesis methods of CDs can be divided into two main types: “top-down” and “bottom-up” approaches [9,10]. In the “top-down” synthesis, carbon precursors are first functionalized/passivated, and then subjected to relatively harsh treatments to cleave bulk carbon materials into quantum dots less than 10 nm in size. The main techniques include electrochemical etching, oxidative acid treatment, laser ablation, and electric arc discharge. In the “bottom-up” approaches, small organic precursors are converted into CDs through hydrothermal/solvothermal method, pyrolysis, microwave-assisted heating, or carbonization, which can achieve surface passivation of CDs in one-pot without separate steps [11,12]. Synthesizing fluorescent CDs from natural products represents a green, cost-effective approach with promising potential utility [13–16], underscoring the importance of investigating their fabrication and applications from natural sources [13–15].

Taraxacum, a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Asteraceae family and Taraxacum genus, is prevalently recognized as a wild vegetable and a traditional Chinese medicinal herb in China. The entire plant is utilized for medicinal purposes, exhibiting efficacies such as clearing heat, detoxifying, reducing swelling, and dispersing lumps. It can be consumed raw, stir-fried, or used to prepare soups, epitomizing a plant that serves both culinary and medicinal functions. In this work, we use the natural biomass dandelion as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source to synthesize CDs through a one-step solvothermal method, and successfully prepare blue, green and orange emissive CDs with different wavelengths via column chromatography separation. The structural, compositional, and optical properties of the as-prepared tricolor CDs are characterized. We also investigate the quantum yields of the CDs in different solvents, demonstrating that the obtained tricolor CDs possess excellent salt tolerance and photostability, accompanied by relatively high quantum yields. Based on the features and advantages of the multicolor CDs, we design and develop novel application techniques to explore their multifunctional applications in information anti-counterfeiting, chemical sensing, and other areas, in order to enhance the application values of the CDs. Finally, metal ion detection is introduced as an example of the practical use of the tricolor CDs. Compared with other multicolor CDs synthesis methods, this approach is more facile and efficient, which is conducive to the mass production of multicolor CDs. This work holds great value for reducing the production costs of CDs, deepening the understanding of CDs photoluminescence mechanisms, overcoming the limitations of analytical techniques based on single-color CDs, and promoting the applications of CDs in information security, analytical detection, bioimaging, and other areas.

Taraxacum (dried) were procured from a local supermarket in Chengdu. P-phenylenediamine and ethanol (analytical grade) were acquired from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. Periodic acid (analytical grade) was obtained from Shanghai App Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. The experimental water utilized was self-made double-distilled water. The hydrothermal reactor (25 mL) was purchased from Xi Niu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was sourced from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric thermostatic blast drying oven was procured from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shunyu Hengping 756PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Prism F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Initially, 10 g of dandelion was pulverized using a food processor, followed by soaking in 200 mL of ethanol for 12 h to extract the necessary components. Subsequently, 5 mL of the dandelion soaking solution was placed into a polytetrafluoroethylene liner, to which 0.6 g of p-phenylenediamine, 0.5 g of periodic acid (serving as the oxidizing agent), and 5 mL of ethanol (acting as the solvent) were added. The mixture was then subjected to ultrasonication for 10 min in an ultrasonic machine, followed by placement in a high-pressure reactor. The reaction was conducted at 180 °C for 6 h. After allowing the mixture to naturally cool to room temperature, the carbon dot solution underwent chromatographic separation, resulting in the tri-color CDs (Fig. 1 ).

To validate that the three types of CDs are derived from the reaction of carbon and nitrogen sources, control experiments were conducted. The carbon source, nitrogen source, and their mixture were reacted under identical conditions. The resulting carbon dot solutions were subjected to spectroscopic testing, with results presented in the following figure. Fig. 2 displays a comparison of the emission spectra, maximum fluorescence intensity, and maximum emission wavelength of CDs prepared from individual carbon and nitrogen sources and their mixture under identical conditions. The results indicate that the fluorescence intensity of CDs prepared from individual carbon and nitrogen sources is extremely low, whereas all three types of CDs prepared from their mixture exhibit considerable intensity. This demonstrates that the three types of CDs are synthesized through the reaction of carbon and nitrogen sources.

The structures and compositions of the three CDs were analyzed using Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM images of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs were depicted in Fig. 3 a–c, respectively. It is evident that the particles of the tricolor CDs are not uniformly distributed, with average sizes of approximately 3.5 nm, 2.6 nm, and 2.3 nm for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, respectively. Additionally, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm in all samples, corresponding to the (100) lattice distance of the graphitic carbon [17].

The FT-IR spectra of the tri-color CDs are shown in Fig. 3d–f. Multiple sharp absorption peaks at 3730 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H or amine N–H; an absorption peak at 2929 cm−1 due to C–H stretching vibrations; an absorption peak at 2355 cm−1 corresponding to CN stretching vibrations; multiple sharp absorption peaks between 1650 cm−1 and 1520 cm−1 corresponding to the vibrational absorptions of CO, C–N, and the phenyl ring CC in amide groups; and an absorption peak at 1074 cm−1 for C–O single bond stretching vibrations. This suggests that the surface of the tri-color CDs may contain amide groups, aldehyde groups, etc.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to analyze the chemical composition and elemental distribution of the three carbon dots. The XPS spectra of the three carbon dots, shown in Fig. 4 a–c, reveal that the primary elements present are C, N, and O. Furthermore, a gradual increase in nitrogen content is observed from B-CDs to O-CDs, suggesting that a higher nitrogen content leads to a red-shift in the fluorescence of the carbon dots. Fig. 4d displays the C1s spectrum of B-CDs, exhibiting three characteristic carbon peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C/CC), 285.4 eV (C–N), and 286.2 eV (C–O). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 4g contains two peaks at 532.3 eV and 533.8 eV, corresponding to the CO and C–O functional groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum in Fig. 4j can be deconvoluted into two sub-peaks at 398.8 eV (C–N–C) and 400.6 eV (CN–C). For G-CDs, the C1s spectrum in Fig. 4e shows three characteristic carbon peaks at 284.6 eV (C–C/CC), 285.2 eV (C–N), and 286.4 eV (C–O). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 4h contains two peaks at 532.4 eV and 533.8 eV, corresponding to the CO and C–O functional groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum in Fig. 4k can be deconvoluted into two sub-peaks at 398.8 eV (C–N–C) and 400.4 eV (CN–C). The C1s spectrum of O-CDs in Fig. 4f exhibits three characteristic carbon peaks at 284.8 eV (C–C/CC), 286.0 eV (C–N), and 288.0 eV (C–O). The O1s spectrum in Fig. 4i shows only one fitted signal peak at 532.0 eV, corresponding to the CO functional group. The high-resolution N1s spectrum in Fig. 4l can be deconvoluted into two sub-peaks at 398.6 eV (C–N–C) and 400.2 eV (CN–C). These results suggest a similar structural composition among the three carbon dots.

Fig. 5 a–c depict the three-dimensional fluorescence spectra of the three CDs, simultaneously providing information on excitation and emission, allowing for a visual assessment of the fluorescence peak positions and numbers. It can be observed that the shapes of the excitation and emission peaks are irregular for all three CDs. Fig. 5d–f presents the three-dimensional contour spectra of the three CDs, with the horizontal and vertical axes representing the emission and excitation wavelengths, respectively. The excitation ranges for B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs are 360–460 nm, 390–490 nm, and 400–560 nm, respectively. B-CDs exhibit the narrowest excitation range, followed by G-CDs, while O-CDs have the broadest excitation range. On the other hand, all three CDs possess relatively narrow emission ranges, spanning 450–550 nm for B-CDs, 480–600 nm for G-CDs, and 550–650 nm for O-CDs. Fig. 5g–i displays the UV–Vis absorption spectra, fluorescence excitation spectra, and emission spectra of the three carbon dots, respectively. These three CDs exhibit absorption peaks at their respective maximum excitation wavelengths, characteristic of their fluorescence, primarily originating from electronic transitions of functional groups on the surface of the CDs [18–20]. From the excitation and emission spectra, it can be observed that the maximum excitation wavelengths for the three CDs are 413 nm, 466 nm, and 467 nm, respectively, while their maximum emission wavelengths are 481 nm, 530 nm, and 585 nm, respectively. The filled colors underneath the emission spectra curves correspond to the fluorescence colors of the respective emission wavelengths, which are blue for B-CDs, green for G-CDs, and orange for O-CDs. Fig. 5j-l illustrate the time-resolved fluorescence spectra and corresponding decay fitting curves for the three carbon dots. As shown, the fluorescence lifetimes of the three CDs are 3.42 ns, 4.08 ns, and 2.97 ns, respectively.

To study excitation dependent emission behavior of the CDs, the fluorescence spectra of the three types of CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths. The results are shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs all exhibit single emission behavior, with the fluorescence intensity reaching a maximum at their respective optimal excitation wavelengths. The fluorescence intensity decreases as the excitation wavelength deviates from the optimal value. This single emission behavior is beneficial for their application in fluorescence imaging, as it avoids the interference of background fluorescence and enhances the imaging contrast.

To enhance and optimize the optical properties of the three types of CDs, an investigation into the fluorescence behavior of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs in various polar solvents was conducted. The carbon dot powders, post-chromatographic drying, were introduced into six solvents of differing polarities (water, acetonitrile, ethanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran, and N,N-dimethylformamide), followed by fluorescence spectroscopy testing of these carbon dot solutions. As depicted in Fig. 7 a–c, it is evident that the solvent polarity exerts a substantial influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, while having a minimal impact on their emission wavelength. For a more lucid comparison of the solvent effects on the fluorescence of the CDs, the maximum fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength of the three types of CDs in different solvents were compiled, with the results showcased in Fig. 7d–i. It is distinctly observable that while the emission wavelength of the CDs remains relatively constant across different solvents, their fluorescence intensity exhibits significant variations. This phenomenon could be attributed to the varying solvent environments, where certain external conditions may disrupt the functional groups and defect states on the surface of the CDs, leading to a reduction in fluorescence intensity. Conversely, other conditions may enhance the surface defect states, thereby augmenting the luminescence performance of the CDs. This provides valuable insights into how to enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs.

To elucidate the influence of solvents on the luminescence properties of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, the quantum yield of the CDs in different solvents was measured. Using acetonitrile, ethanol, acetone, tetrahydrofuran, N,N-dimethylformamide, and water as solvents, the fluorescence spectra and absorbance of the CDs were measured across varying concentrations. Subsequently, plots of integrated fluorescence intensity against absorbance were constructed, as illustrated in Fig. 8 (a–c). Utilizing quinine sulfate as a standard, the relative quantum yields of the three types of CDs in different solvents were calculated. The results, presented in Fig. 8(d–f), reveal variations in the quantum yield of the CDs across different solvents. Specifically, the quantum yield of B-CDs is highest in THF at 43.8 %, G-CDs in EtOH at 32.9 %, and O-CDs in ACN at 48.1 %. These findings underscore that the blue, green, and orange CDs synthesized in this study possess relatively high quantum yields. Table 1 presents quantum yields of some fluorescent carbon dots synthesized using natural products as reported in the literature. It is evident that our carbon dots demonstrate outstanding quantum yields, surpassing many other carbon dots.

Ions such as sodium and potassium play a crucial role in maintaining salt balance within biological organisms. Therefore, this experiment investigated the impact of different concentrations of NaCl solutions on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, simulating the internal environment of biological entities. NaCl solutions of concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 mM were prepared and added to solutions of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs with identical fluorescence intensities. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, it is observed that as the concentration of NaCl solution increases from 0 mM to 1000 mM, there is no significant change in the fluorescence peak position and peak intensity of the three types of CDs. This indicates that NaCl has almost no effect on the luminescence properties of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs, and they maintain excellent fluorescence performance even in high-concentration NaCl environments.

Given that most practical applications require the fluorescence of CDs to possess good resistance to photobleaching, the photostability of the three types of CDs was investigated. Three CDs solutions were continuously irradiated with a 365 nm UV lamp from a distance of 5 cm for 2 h, with the fluorescence intensity of the solutions tested every 20 min. The results are presented in Fig. 9b. After 2 h of UV irradiation, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs decreased by approximately 8.9 %, 10.5 %, and 5.3 %, respectively, with the largest decrease in fluorescence intensity being only 10.5 %. This demonstrates that the three types of CDs synthesized in this experiment possess excellent resistance to photobleaching, enabling their use in complex optical environments.

In practical applications, CDs are subjected to various temperature environments, necessitating good thermal stability to cope with these complex and variable conditions. To study the impact of temperature on the CDs, they were placed in environments ranging from 30 °C to 60 °C for 3 min, and their emission spectra were measured. The results are shown in Fig. 10 a-c. For a more intuitive comparison of the temperature effects on the CDs, the maximum fluorescence intensity of the CDs at different temperatures was compiled, with the results presented in Fig. 10d. It is clearly observed that as the temperature gradually increases from 30 °C to 60 °C, a slight decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the three types of CDs occurs, with the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs decreasing by approximately 4.9 %, 3.3 %, and 10.3 %, respectively. This could be attributed to temperature variations affecting the surface electron configuration and molecular structure of the CDs, leading to a decrease in fluorescence intensity. However, the overall decrease is minimal, indicating that the three CDs exhibit excellent thermal stability under extreme conditions.

In practical applications, it is imperative for CDs to be storable over long periods while maintaining fluorescence stability. To validate the stability of the CDs, the three types were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for seven consecutive days. The fluorescence spectra of the CDs before and after the seven days were tested, with the results shown in Fig. 11 a–c. After seven days, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs decreased by approximately 13 %, 11 %, and 8 %, respectively. The decrease in fluorescence intensity for all three types of CDs was within a reasonable range, demonstrating their excellent stability and suitability for long-term storage and use.

Given the biological roles of metal ions and their toxicity to the environment, human health, plants, and animals when exceeding certain thresholds, it is imperative to detect metal ions and effectively monitor their concentrations in aqueous solutions and various biological samples. CDs have garnered significant attention in the field of metal ion probe detection, demonstrating responsiveness to a majority of metal ions. To evaluate the selectivity of the three types of CDs towards metal ions, their fluorescence emission intensities were studied in the presence of various metal ions. 1 mL of different metal ion solutions (20 μM) were added to 1 mL of carbon dot solutions, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded, as shown in Fig. 12 a–c. The degree of fluorescence quenching was characterized using the relative fluorescence intensity I/I 0, with results presented in Fig. 12d–f. It is evident that these metal ions have varying effects on the fluorescence intensities of the three CDs, with B-CDs showing selectivity towards Fe3+, G-CDs towards Pb2+, and O-CDs towards Sn4+. Fig. 12g–i illustrates the impact of adding Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+ on the relative fluorescence intensity I/I 0 in the mixed systems of metal ions and CDs. The bar graphs reveal that the addition of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+ still significantly reduces the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs in the mixed systems, while other metal ions do not affect the quenching effect of these three metal ions on CDs. This confirms that the prepared CDs possess excellent ion anti-interference capabilities.

Solutions of Fe3+ and B-CDs at varying concentrations (0–80 μM) were prepared and mixed thoroughly, and their fluorescence spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 13 a. With increasing concentrations of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs decreased. The relationship between the degree of fluorescence quenching (I 0/I) and the concentration of metal ions can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation, as depicted in Fig. 13d. Within the concentration range of 0–80 μM for Fe3+, there is a good linear relationship between the degree of fluorescence quenching of the quantum dots and the concentration of Fe3+, with the linear regression equation (I 0-I)/I = 0.0191C(Fe3+) + 0.0886 and a correlation coefficient R 2 = 0.9942. The detection limit for Fe3+ was calculated using 3 times the standard deviation of the blank value divided by the slope (3σ/k), resulting in 1.8 μM, with a standard deviation of 1.64 %. Similarly, the linear equations for G-CDs and O-CDs are (I 0-I)/I = 0.1482C(Pb2+) + 0.0695 (Fig. 13e) and (I 0-I)/I = 0.4068C(Sn4+) + 0.1116 (Fig. 13f), with detection limits for Pb2+ and Sn4+ being 0.5 μM and 0.1 μM, respectively. The iron content in the national drinking water hygiene standard (GB 5749-2006) is specified as 0.3 mg/L (5 μM); the lead content in the national electroplating pollutant discharge standard (GB 21900-2008) is specified as 0.2 mg/L (0.8 μM); the tin content in the national industrial pollutant discharge standard (GB 30770-2014) is specified as 2 mg/L (16 μM), all of which are higher than the detection limits of the methods described in this paper, proving that these three types of CDs can be applied to the detection of actual samples. As shown in Table 2 , the three fluorescent probes prepared in this study exhibit low detection limits for Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+, surpassing the performance of methods reported in most literature.

The fluorescence quenching of carbon dots by metal ions may be related to the functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots. The functional groups on the carbon dot surface undergo coordination with metal ions, forming complexes, which leads to the quenching of the carbon dot fluorescence. Different carbon dots can coordinate with different metal cations, enabling these three carbon dots to detect different metal cations. To investigate the detection mechanisms of these three fluorescent probes, the UV–Vis absorption spectra and infrared spectra of the three fluorescent carbon dots were measured before and after the addition of metal ions.

Fig. 14 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs, Fe3+@B-CDs, and Fe3+. In the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of B-CDs, the peak at 280 nm disappears after the addition of Fe3+, and a new broad absorption peak appears in the range of 300–400 nm for the Fe3+@B-CDs mixture solution. The peak of B-CDs in the 400–500 nm range also undergoes a red shift. Furthermore, in the infrared spectrum shown in Fig. 14d, the O–H stretching vibration peak at 3730 cm−1 is significantly weakened in the Fe3+@B-CDs mixture system compared to the isolated B-CDs, while other peak values remain unchanged. This suggests that upon the addition of Fe3+, the hydroxyl functional groups on the B-CDs surface coordinate with Fe3+, forming a ground-state complex attached to the carbon dot surface, affecting the luminescence properties of B-CDs and leading to the quenching effect by Fe3+, which is a static quenching process.

Fig. 14b shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of G-CDs, Pb2+@G-CDs, and Pb2+. It can be observed that after the addition of Pb2+, the absorption peak of G-CDs at 300 nm disappears, and a new peak appears at 480 nm. Fig. 14e shows the infrared spectra of G-CDs and Pb2+@G-CDs. In the Pb2+@G-CDs mixture system, the N–H stretching vibration peak at 3443 cm−1 and the C–N absorption peak at 1386 cm−1 are significantly weakened compared to the isolated G-CDs, while other peak values do not show significant changes. This indicates that the amino groups on the G-CDs surface undergo coordination with Pb2+. These coordination interactions alter the electronic structure of G-CDs, affecting the distribution of electrons and accelerating non-radiative recombination, forming a new non-fluorescent coordination compound through a static quenching process, leading to the quenching of G-CDs fluorescence.

Fig. 14c shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of O-CDs, Sn4+@O-CDs, and Sn4+. From the figure, it can be observed that the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Sn4+ in the range of 200–600 nm does not exhibit any absorption peaks, indicating the absence of defects. After the addition of Sn4+ to O-CDs, a new absorption peak appears in the range of 500–600 nm, undergoing a red shift compared to the absorption peak of O-CDs at 500 nm. In the infrared spectrum shown in Fig. 14f, the O–H stretching vibration peak at 3710 cm−1 is significantly weakened in the Sn4+@O-CDs mixture system compared to the isolated O-CDs. This suggests that upon the addition of Sn4+, the hydroxyl functional groups on the O-CDs surface coordinate with Sn4+, forming a new non-fluorescent coordination compound through a static quenching process, leading to the quenching of O-CDs fluorescence.

To further validate the effectiveness of CDs in detecting metal ions, laboratory wastetap water was tested to verify the accuracy of this method in analyzing actual water samples. The results are presented in Table 3 . The reliability of the analytical method was determined through spike recovery experiments. The spike recovery rate of Fe3+ in actual water samples ranged from 99.5 % to 102.2 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ≤ 10.8 %; for Pb2+, the spike recovery rate ranged from 95.9 % to 98.5 %, with an RSD ≤8.62 %; for Sn4+, the spike recovery rate ranged from 98.1 % to 100.5 %, with an RSD ≤7.98 %. The results indicate that the three types of CDs prepared in this experiment possess sufficient reliability and sensitivity, and can be applied to the detection of water samples in actual environmental conditions.

In this work, we successfully synthesized three types of CDs capable of emitting blue, green, and orange light using dandelion as the carbon source, o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and ethanol as the solvent, through a one-step solvothermal method, followed by successful separation via chromatography. The structural and optical properties of these CDs were characterized and studied. These CDs exhibited excellent salt resistance, photobleaching resistance, and thermal stability. All three types of CDs demonstrated single emission behavior, indicating unique luminescent centers. They also exhibited strong luminescent capabilities, with quantum yields of 43.8 %, 32.9 %, and 48.1 %, respectively. In the analysis of metal ion detection, B-CDs, G-CDs, and O-CDs showed high selective quenching responses to Fe3+, Pb2+, and Sn4+, respectively, with good linearity and low detection limits, making them applicable in actual samples. Overall, this work provides a simple and efficient method for synthesizing multicolor CDs with high quantum yields, expanding the practical applications of CDs.

Xiang Long: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Shihua Liao: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiayang Wang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yanshun Ma: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
